coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of nitrogen, phosphorus-doped Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots for colorimetric/fluorometric dual-modal nitrite assay with a portable smartphone platform
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Yuxuan
He, Yu
Wang, Manman
Song, Gongwu
coredata.dc:description: Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots (Ti3C2 MQDs) are recognized as promising new nanomaterial recently, while the majority of the Ti3C2 MQDs manifest limited maximum emission in the blue-light range. Herein, the green fluorescence nitrogen, phosphorus-doped Ti3C2 MQDs (N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs) were synthesized via facile microwave-assisted method, which greatly reduces the reaction time. The as-prepared N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs showed excitation-dependent photoluminescence, anti-photobleaching, and favorable dispersibility. Interestingly, the fluorescence of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs can be quenched by 1,10-phenanthroline-Fe (II) complex (Phen-Fe2+) via inner filter effect (IFE), accompanied by the increased orange color. After introduction of nitrite (NO2 -), owing to the redox reaction between NO2 - and Fe2+, the fluorescence will be recovered and the color varies from orange to colorless gradually. Therefore, a colorimetric/fluorometric dual-modal recognition of NO2 - was fabricated based on N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs and Phen-Fe2+ complex. Moreover, paper-based test trips were facilely made by the N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs/Phen-Fe2+ sensing system in response to ultrafast-response, real-time detection of NO2 - with the assistant of smartphone. This study tendered a novel dual-channel method for real-time visual assay of NO2 - as well as provided insightful research into functionalized Ti3C2 MQDs regarding in food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ti3C2 Mxene quantum dots
Nitrite
Fluorescence
Colorimetry
Test paper
Smartphone sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of NO2 - in sausage samples
Preparation of the visual test papers for NO2 - determination
Characterization and photoluminescence properties of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs
Optimization of conditions of Phen-Fe2+ complex-induced fluorescence quenching
Design of the colorimetric/fluorometric dual-modal sensor
Colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of NO2 -

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Wuhan Youth Science and Technology Plan
State Key Laboratory of Biocatalysis and Enzyme Engineering
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21707030 ), Wuhan Youth Science and Technology Plan ( 2016070204010133 ) and Open Project Funding of the State Key Laboratory of Biocatalysis and Enzyme Engineering ( SKLBEE2020017 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM (inset HRTEM) image, (B) the size distribution, (C) full XPS spectra and (D) excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of the prepared N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs.

(A) Selectivity of Phen toward different metal ions (metal ions from left to right in the photograph: K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Fe3+). N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs, 0.08 mg mL−1; ion concentration, 75 μM. (B) Effects of complex ratio of Fe2+ to Phen to fluorescence quenching. (C) Fluorescence spectra of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs after adding different concentrations of Phen-Fe2+ complex (the Fe2+ concentration from 0 to 100 μM, the Phen concentration from 0 to 300 μM). (D) Effects of incubation time to fluorescence quenching. Experimental conditions: N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs, 0.1 mg mL−1; Phen, 225 μM; Fe2+, 75 μM; H+, 40 μM.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Phen-Fe2+ (red line) and excitation and emission spectrum of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs. (B) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of the N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs and the N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs-Phen-Fe2+. (C) Feasibility of the NO2 - detection by the proposed sensor: fluorescence spectra of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs. Inset showed the corresponding fluorescent photographs under a 395 nm UV light. (D) UV−vis spectra and image (inset) of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs in the addition of Phen, Phen+Fe2+ and Phen+Fe2++NO2 -, respectively. Experimental conditions: N, P-Ti3C2.

. (A) The FL spectra of the system in the presence of different concentration of NO2 − (0–100 μM). (B) The linear plot of FL intensity ratio (F-F0)/F0 versus the concentration of NO2 − (1.5–80 μM). The inset: the corresponding photographs of the system with different concentration of NO2 − taken under 395 UV light (from left to right: 1.5, 15, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80 μM). (C) Absorption spectra of the proposed system upon the addition of NO2 − at different concentrations (0–110 μM). (D) The linear plot of absorbance ratio (A0-A)/A0 versus the concentration of NO2 − (4–85 μM). The inset: the corresponding photographs of the system with different concentration of NO2 − taken under daylight (from left to right: 4, 15, 30, 40, 55,70, 85 μM). Sensing system: N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs, 0.1 mg mL−1; Phen, 225 μM; Fe2+,75 μM; H+, 40 μM.
Selectivity of N, P -Ti3C2 MQDs/Phen/Fe2+ system toward different interfering substances, investigated by (A) FL spectra and (B) UV–vis spectra.

(A) Application of the smartphone-assisted sensing NO2 -. (B) The photographs of the colorimetric test papers with increased concentrations of NO2 - (from left to right: 0, 10, 25, 40, 55, 70, and 80 μM). (C) The curve of plotting R/G intensity against different NO2 - concentration.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis for N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs and dual-modal strategy for the detection of NO2 -.
Determination of NO2 − in sausage samples.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of nitrogen, phosphorus-doped Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots for colorimetric/fluorometric dual-modal nitrite assay with a portable smartphone platform

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, North China University of Science and Technology, No.21 Bohai Road, Caofeidian, Tangshan 063210, Hebei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Public Health, North China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Public Health, North China University of Science and Technology, No.21 Bohai Road, Caofeidian, Tangshan 063210, Hebei, China. Tel: +(86) 03158805585

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots (Ti3C2 MQDs) are recognized as promising new nanomaterial recently, while the majority of the Ti3C2 MQDs manifest limited maximum emission in the blue-light range. Herein, the green fluorescence nitrogen, phosphorus-doped Ti3C2 MQDs (N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs) were synthesized via facile microwave-assisted method, which greatly reduces the reaction time. The as-prepared N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs showed excitation-dependent photoluminescence, anti-photobleaching, and favorable dispersibility. Interestingly, the fluorescence of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs can be quenched by 1,10-phenanthroline-Fe (II) complex (Phen-Fe2+) via inner filter effect (IFE), accompanied by the increased orange color. After introduction of nitrite (NO2 -), owing to the redox reaction between NO2 - and Fe2+, the fluorescence will be recovered and the color varies from orange to colorless gradually. Therefore, a colorimetric/fluorometric dual-modal recognition of NO2 - was fabricated based on N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs and Phen-Fe2+ complex. Moreover, paper-based test trips were facilely made by the N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs/Phen-Fe2+ sensing system in response to ultrafast-response, real-time detection of NO2 - with the assistant of smartphone. This study tendered a novel dual-channel method for real-time visual assay of NO2 - as well as provided insightful research into functionalized Ti3C2 MQDs regarding in food analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 -) is a significant substance as food preservative and fertilizer which is widely utilized in food industry [1]. However, high levels of NO2 - has been proven to be a serious threat to the public health [2]. Severe human diseases including cancer, muscular paralysis, and neurodegenerative illness are connected with the intake of NO2 -. The carcinogenic N-nitrosamines compounds could be generated in vivo from absorbed NO2 - during the physiological process [3,4]. International Agency for Research on Cancer of the World Health Organization has classified NO2 - as 2 A carcinogens [5]. The maximum surplus of NO2 - in meat products is regulated to be 50 mg/kg by the European Union [6]. Therefore, exploiting new methods to detect NO2 - is worthy of considerable attention and investigation.

In general, many conventional approaches for the detection of trace NO2 - have been achieved, including chromatography [7,8], electrochemistry [9,10], and chemiluminescence [11,12]. Most reported methods are time-consuming, complicated in operation, high in cost and poor in portability, which is not suitable for practical applications. Owing to their advantages in low cost, rapid response and easy operation, fluorometric and colorimetric strategies are identified as most potential to develop portable sensors for the detection of NO2 - [13,14]. With the growth of fluorescent materials, several fluorescent probes for the detection of NO2 - have been fabricated like carbon quantum dots [15], composite nanoparticles [16], gold nanoclusters [17]. Simultaneously, development in the use of colorimetric detection of NO2 - have been witnessed in recent years [18–20]. The portability and practicality of smartphones have made a contribution to the quantitative analysis [21]. To improve the accuracy and sensitivity, only a few dual-model (fluorescence and colorimetric) measurements have been explored with the assistant of smartphone, which can transform color information into data information to achieve the accurate quantitative detection of NO2 - [22]. Hence, there will be a promising prospect to develop dual-modal sensing platform for the detection of NO2 - with the assistant of smartphone.

MXenes is an extensively concerned 2-dimensional (2D) layered nanomaterial, including Ti3C2, Ti2C, Nb2C and Ta4C3Tx etc., for which various fields of research believe it a revolutionary 2D material [23,24]. The Ti3C2 MXene-derived quantum dots (Ti3C2 MQDs) via cutting MXene can further show improved dispersibility, higher conductivity, easier modification and unique luminescence [25,26]. Therefore, Ti3C2 MQDs are ideal candidate materials for optoelectronics, enzymatic, cancer therapy, biosensing and bioimaging application [27–30]. The research on Ti3C2 MQDs is still in its infancy, especially their most fascinating optical properties are still investigated less than expected. Most reported Ti3C2 MQDs exhibit intensive emission in blue region, which significantly limit their widespread application in many fields [31–33]. The vital factors of optical property regulations are containing particle size and heteroatom doping as well as reaction solvent and precursors, which are crucial in processing of Ti3C2 MQDs [34–36]. Furthermore, the reports on Ti3C2 MQDs synthesis are generally through the delamination of multilayered MXene via troublesome and time-consuming hydrothermal methods [37,38]. Therefore, it is imperative to take efforts in novel straightforward synthesis of Ti3C2 MQDs with strong emission at a longer wavelength for broaden application.

Our group put forward microwave-assisted Ti3C2 MQDs synthesis method for the first time before, which is convenient, efficient and fast compared with hydrothermal method [39]. However, the Ti3C2 MQDs show blue fluorescence with the emission at 430 nm like the Ti3C2 MQDs prepared by other methods [40]. On this basis, N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs with highly green fluorescence were fabricated by microwave-assisted irradiation with phosphoric acid in formamide solvent. The as-obtained Ti3C2 MQDs possessed superb quantum yield (QY) of up to 21.5% with green emission, which is greatly higher than Ti3C2 MQDs prepared in other solvents. Herein, a colorimetric/fluorometric dual-modal sensor for ultrasensitive determination of NO2 - was introduced based on N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs/Phen-Fe2+ system with the assistant of smartphone ( Scheme 1). The fluorescence intensity was quenched by Phen-Fe2+ complex due to inner filter effect (IFE). Upon the addition of NO2 -, the redox reaction between NO2 - and Fe2+ to generate Fe3+, which decrease the effect of IFE. The change of color and fluorescence responding to NO2 - happened at the same time. The system also served a kind of visual paper strip. A series of color variation of the paper strip can be observed by naked eyes with introduction of different concentrations of NO2 -. Then the smartphone could read out the corresponding Red (R), Green (G) and Blue (B) values by a Color Recognizer app installed in the smartphone to accomplish quantitative analysis. The combination of test strips and smartphone to analyze NO2 - is meet the need of simple implementation of visualization assay on site within short time less than10 mins. The sensing strategy demonstrates wider detection range (4–85 μM by colorimetry and 1.5–80 μM by fluorescence method), ultrasensitive and ultrafast-response, and it shows positive results for real sausage samples. Therefore, the green fluorescence N, P- doped Ti3C2 MQDs not only endow good hydrophilicity and biocompatibility but also can be conducted an ingenious sensing towards to NO2 -, which broadens the application scope of MQDs. As to our best knowledge, this work states a facile synthesis of MXene-derived QDs and, for the first time, combine N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs and Phen/Fe2+ as a sensing probe to accomplish dual-modal for detection of NO2 - with the assistant of smartphone.

Ti3AlC2 powders were provided by Institute of Metal Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences. 1,10-Phenanthroline (99%) and ammonium hydroxide were purchased from Sinopharm chemical Regent Co. Ltd (Beijing, China). FeSO4.7 H2O, FeCl3.6 H2O, KBr, KI, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, arginine (Arg), tryptophan (Try), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), aspartic acid (Asp), serine (Ser), histidine (His), cysteine (Cys), alanine (Alan) were obtained from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. CH3COONa·3H2O, Na2B4O7·10H2O, H3PO4, NaF, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, NaNO2, Na3PO4, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, BaCl2, ZnCl2, MnCl2 and formamide were acquired from Aladdin Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China) and NaNO3 was supplied by Bodi Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). All reagents were in analytical grade and used as received without purification.

The XD-100A computer microwave catalytic synthetic extractor (Beijing Xianghu Technology Co., Ltd., China) was applied to synthesize N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was taken with a Spectrum one FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, America). The size and morphology of the prepared Ti3C2 MQDs were represented on a JEM-2100 UHR microscope (JEOL, Japan) and Talos-200 FEI HRTEM (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Xray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed with a VGEscalab 200 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were obtained by a D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The UV–vis spectra and Fluorescence spectra were analyzed using a Lambda 35 UV spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, America) and a LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, America), respectively. The fluorescence lifetimes were acquired on a FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, Britain).

Firstly, the thin-layered Ti3C2 MXene was obtained by HF etching method we previously reported. Subsequently, the acquired Ti3C2 MXene (40 mg), 1.8 mL H3PO4 and 25 mL formamide were added to a three-necked flask in the microwave reactor, adding ammonia until the pH 9.0. The reaction conditions were 25 min of time, 120 ℃ of temperature, and 800 W of the power. After centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant was filtered by 220 nm filtration membrane to obtain the products. Finally, after rotary evaporation and vacuum drying, N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs powders were obtained and stored under 6 ℃ for subsequent use.

In a typical process of detecting NO2 -, varied concentration of NO2 - standard solution and 75 μL Fe2+ solution (2 mM) and 40 μL H+ solution (2 mM) were incubated at 37 ℃ for 10 min. Afterwards, 225 μL Phen solution (2 mM) and 200 μL N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs (1 mg mL−1) were added into the above mixture and the final volume was diluted to 2 mL, allowing to sustain for 20 min. Eventually, the fluorescence measured under the excitation at 390 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NO2 - in sausage samples
ce:section.ce:para: All the sausage products were taken from nearby supermarket in Wuhan (China), and sausage samples were pretreated. Each 15 g sample was accurately weighed and placed in a flask, with manual grinding. Then the mixture was stirred with 37.5 mL saturated borax solution and 300 mL hot distilled water. Next, it was heated for 20 min and cooled to room temperature. 15 mL of ZnSO4 (1.0 M) was subsequently added to the above compounds to remove proteins, and followed by centrifugation. Finally, the intermediate fraction was filtered through 220 nm filter and the volume was adjusted to 500 mL, storing at 4 ℃ [41,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the visual test papers for NO2 - determination
ce:section.ce:para: Fiber filter papers were fabricated to realize visual analysis of NO2 - on site. Briefly, 0.1 mg mL−1 N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs and 225 μM Phen solution were dropped onto filter strip and allowed to dry naturally. Then various concentration of NO2 -, 75 μL Fe2+ solution (2 mM) and 40 μL H+ solution (2 mM) were diluted to final volume 2 mL, incubated for 10 mins. After that, taking 10 μL of above solution and adding to the dried filter paper. The colorimetric as-prepared test strips can be observed by naked eyes in sunlight. The color recognizer app stalled in smartphone can help us capture the R (red), G (green), B (blue) values of proposed test strips.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and photoluminescence properties of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimum reaction conditions for the synthesis of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs were explored in Fig. S1. Consequently, 30 min and pH 9 were selected as optimal condition for the preparation. The morphology and size of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs were illustrated through TEM and HRTEM. Fig. 1A characterized that the prepared N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs exhibited a uniform dimension and the well-dispersed particles with an average diameter of 3.11 ± 0.86 nm (Fig. 1B). HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1 A) further confirmed the prominent crystallization of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs with sequential lattice fringes spacing at 0.308 nm. XPS was used to analyze the surface chemical compositions and electronic states of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs, six main peaks at 285.5, 401.3, 532.3, 134, 192 and 457.9 eV were ascribed to the elements of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, P 2p, P 2 s and Ti 2p, respectively (Fig. 1C). The narrow-scan spectra of C, N, P, and Ti are displayed in Fig. S2. To further verify the surface functional groups, N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs was characterized by the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3A). The typical band appeared near 3100–3400 cm−1 were attributed to the -OH/-NH2 bonds stretching vibration. The peaks 1727 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 were assigned to CO and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively. The bands centered at 1400 cm−1 and 1280 cm−1 were due to C-O and C-N. Particularly, the peaks at 768 cm−1 and 551 cm−1 may be due to the vibration of the Ti-O and Ti-C bonds. The above results declared that N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs are successfully acquired.

The photoluminescence characteristics of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs were collected by UV−vis and fluorescence (FL) spectra. As clarified in Fig. S3B, a peak at 270 nm and an absorption band at 300 nm were observed. The N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs exhibited a maximum FL emission wavelength at 500 nm under the optimal excitation at 390 nm (Fig. 1D). Additionally, the as-prepared N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs showed bright green FL under 395 nm ultraviolet lamp (Inset of Fig. 1D). It can be found that the excitation-dependent fluorescence characteristic of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs, presenting the emission wavelength red-shifted as the excitation wavelength increased. As shown in Fig. S4, Ti3C2 MQDs exhibited bright green luminescence only when prepared with formamide solvent under the irradiation of 395 nm ultraviolet lamp. This result suggests that formamide (or its decomposed outcomes) predominantly take part in the constitution of the N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs. The relative QY of obtained N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs was measured to be 21.5%. The positive high QY is attributed to the N and P elements co-doping to regulate structures and compositions of MQDs. Meanwhile, it may arise from the synergy influence surface chemistry, edge effect and lateral size of MQDs in diverse reaction solvent. Therefore, we infer that formamide served both as a solvent and nitrogen source to cause high QY. The stability of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs was investigated (Fig. S5), the FL intensities remained almost the same under different cases containing salt ionic strength, UV irradiation time, solution pH and storage time, indicating N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs possessed good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions of Phen-Fe2+ complex-induced fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, the Phen-Fe2+ complex was built for there exist low-lying π*-orbitals in Phen, promoting them likely to organize metal complexes with metal ions. To prove specificity between Phen and Fe2+, the fluorescence emission of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs to the complex of Phen and different metal ions were testified ( Fig. 2A). The results illustrated that only Fe2+ could explicitly bind to Phen to obtain an orange formation (Phen-Fe2+), quenching the fluorescence of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs at 500 nm dramatically. The optimum quenching conditions were investigated. As the molar ratio Fe2+ to Phen was 1:3, the fluorescence quenching degree reached the maximum (Fig. 2B). After the concentration ratio between Fe2+ and Phen was fixed at 1:3, Fe2+ concentration of 75 μM and a Phen concentration of 225 μM were chosen for the subsequent study. With the increases of the Fe2+ concentration from 0 μM to 100 μM, quenching reached an equilibrium when the Fe2+ concentration was 75 μM (Fig. 2C). The incubation time of reaction between Phen-Fe2+ and N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs was selected as 10 min, for the quenching efficiency was ultimate and balanced (Fig. 2D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the colorimetric/fluorometric dual-modal sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The FL quenching mechanism of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs with the complex of Phen-Fe2+ is demonstrated. The UV−vis absorption spectra of Phen, Phen+Fe2+ and Phen+Fe3+ were displayed in Fig. S6. Phen and Phen+Fe3+ showed no absorbance when the wavelength from 400 nm to 700 nm, while the complex of Phen+Fe2+ displayed absorption peak at 520 nm with orange color (red line in Fig. S6). Fig. 3A displays that the emission spectrum of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs overlap with the absorption spectrum of Phen-Fe2+, declaring that the FL quenching may arise from the IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). As shown in Fig. 3B, fluorescence lifetime decays curves of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs with and without Phen-Fe2+ were recorded. The average lifetime of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs remains unchanged in the absence or presence of Phen-Fe2+, which illustrate that the quenching mechanism of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs caused by Phen-Fe2+ derived from IFE.

The feasibility of the proposed sensor is also discussed based on that only Fe2+ and Phen can form orange complex of Phen-Fe2+. The fluorescence emission of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs at 500 nm was unchanged in the presence of Phen (yellow line and orange line in Fig. 3C). After the introduction of the complex of Phen-Fe2+, the fluorescence of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs was seriously quenching (blue line in Fig. 3C) because of IFE, along with green fluorescence of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs became weaker under 395 UV light (inset of Fig. 3C). After NO2 - was added in the system, the Fe2+ transferred to Fe3+ lead that complex of Phen-Fe2+ broken up along with the decrease of the absorbance at 520 nm (blue line in Fig. 3D). Therefore, the overlap between fluorescence of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs and absorbance of Phen-Fe2+ decreased and quenched fluorescence of N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs was recovering partly (green line in Fig. 3C). As the concentration of NO2 - increased, the color of the system changed from orange to colorless (inset 3D). Thus, it makes possible for us to determine NO2 - with the unaided eyes and further construct a dual-modal recognition of NO2 –strategy.
MQDs, 0.1 mg mL−1; Phen, 225 μM; Fe2+, 75 μM; H+, 40 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of NO2 -
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal sensing conditions were explored. In the process of sensing, the incubation time of NO2 - and Phen/ N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs was selected as 20 min (Fig. S7A). NO2 - is oxidizing under acidic conditions, so it is inevitable to discuss the concentration of H+ concentration. When H+ concentration was 40 μM, the degree of fluorescence recovery arrived equilibrium (Fig. S7B). Thus 40 μM was chosen for optimal H+ concentration for NO2 - determination. Under the optimal conditions, the sensitivity of our method is evaluated. The FL response of N, P -Ti3C2 MQDs/Phen/Fe2+ system gradually recover as the NO2 - concentration increased ( Fig. 4A). The regression equation is expressed as (F-F0)/F0 = 0.0274 [NO2 -]+ 0.1556 (R2 = 0.996) in the range of NO2 − from 1.5 to 80 μM (Fig. 4B), where F0 and F refer to the FL intensity of the system in the absence and presence of NO2 -, respectively. Additionally, the limit of detection (LOD) is determined to be 0.25 μM based on the 3σ/K principle.

Furthermore, as displayed in Fig. 4C, with the increase of NO2 -, the UV–vis absorption peak of the N, P -Ti3C2 MQDs/Phen/Fe2+ is declining, togther with the color of solution variation tendency from orange to pink to almost colorless (inset of Fig. 4D). There is a satisfactory linear relationship between the colorimetric ratio (A0-A)/A0 and the concentrations of NO2 - from 4 to 85 μM with the linear regression equation is (A0-A)/A0 = −0.0043 [NO2 -] + 0.4982, R2 = 0.998 (Fig. 4D). And the results reveal that the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.71 μM. Herein, A0 and A represent the absorbance of the proposed system in the absence and presence of NO2 -, respectively. Compared to those of other strategies, the as-established dual-modal assay for NO2 - exhibit sensitive and broad linear detection range as well as more convincing rather than single signal assay. (Table S1).

To monitor the specificity of the N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs/Phen-Fe2+ sensing system, a range of common anions (F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, NO3 -, CO3 2-, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, HPO4 2-), cations (Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, K+, Zn2+, Ba2+) and coexisting substances (Arg, Try, Gly, Lys, Asp, Ser, His, Cys, Ala) were investigated. As illustrated in Figs. 5 and S8, only introduction of NO2 - to the probe can trigger response, either in the UV absorbance or the fluorescence, which states NO2 −has highly specificity to the proposed system. In addition, in the anti-interference experiment, the absorbance and FL index have little change after adding different interfering substances upon NO2 -. The above results confirm that this approach has remarkable selectivity and anti-interference toward NO2 - assay.

To simplify the assay procedure and realize instrument-free detection on site, the portable colorimetric test strips was established. The smartphone-assisted platform which is consisted of filter papers containing N, P -Ti3C2 MQDs/Phen/Fe2+ solution probe, and a smartphone with the Color Recognition app was utilized both as signal reader and analyzer for the proposed strips ( Fig. 6A). Similarly, as the concentration of NO2 - increased, the relevant color of test strips shifted from deep orange to weak, rendering a good chance to achieve point-of-use analysis by the naked eyes as well as portable device (Fig. 6B). The red channel (R) and green channel (G) values acquired by Color Recognizer app were used to figure the RGB ratio for detecting the NO2 -. Delightedly, the related RGB ratio (R/G) is linearly with the NO2 - concentration over the range of 0–80 μM (R2 = 0.992), and the linear equation is indicated as R/G = −0.0047x + 1.400 (Fig. 6C). The results indicate that the smartphone-assisted colorimetric filter papers are capable of low cost, ease of handling detection NO2 -, exhibiting great potential as forceful analytical tools for on-site sensing.

The remarkable sensitivity and favorable selectivity of the constructed sensor motivated us to evaluate its capacity for practical utilization. Table 1 shows the NO2 − level in six types sausage samples measured by the proposed method and the Griess-based method, respectively. The values earned from the two methods did not show notably different. Determination of NO2 - content in sausage samples by spike recovery, and the results were stated in Table S2, the recovery range from 94.4% to 105.7% and the corresponding relative standard deviation (RSD) values are less than 3.0%. These data point out that this method is reliable for NO2 - determination in practical employment in real samples.

In our work, we have synthesized strong green fluorescence N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs, utilizing time-effective, and facile microwave-assisted methods by formamide serving as solvent and doping agent synchronously. The prepared N, P-Ti3C2 MQDs exhibited fantastic dispersion stability and were further combined with Phen-Fe2+ to realize dual-modal sensing for NO2 -. The colorimetric and fluorometric dual-channel method has several superiorities over traditional method in high accuracy, reliability and selectivity. The above probe based on paper strips are successfully prepared for on-site and easy fabrication of visualization detection with the assist of digital image analysis. Therefore, the proposed work not only develops an ultrasensitive assay for NO2 - but also underway to regulate the optical properties of Ti3C2 MQDs and other MXene-derived MQDs, it is envisioned that there will be more advancement in growth of the MQDs-based fluorescent/optical biosensors.

Yuxuan Bai: Conception and design of the study, Acquisition and analysis of data, Drafting and revising the article. Yu He: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Manman Wang: Data curation, Investigation. Gongwu Song: Validation, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21707030), Wuhan Youth Science and Technology Plan (2016070204010133) and Open Project Funding of the State Key Laboratory of Biocatalysis and Enzyme Engineering (SKLBEE2020017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131410.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuxuan Bai obtained a B.Sc degree in chemical engineering and technology from Hubei Normal University, China in 2019 and she is currently a graduate student in Hubei University. Her research interests include the design of functional fluorescence nanomateials sensors.

Yu He received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from Nankai University, China in 2009. Up to 2010, she worked as postdoctoral fellow at Nanyang Technological University, Singapore and she is currently working as an associate professor in Hubei University, China. Her research interests are mainly in the areas of construction of functional fluorescent nanomaterials and biosensors.
Manman Wang is a professor in School of Public Health at North China University of Science and Technology, China. Her current interests focus on analytical chemistry.
Gongwu Song is a professor in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Hubei University, China. His current interests mainly focus on analytical chemistry and environmental chemistry.
